revision.comment: history
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = | width = 250 | caption = | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1734-1 Miyashiromine
[[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. ==History== This shrine was recorded in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'' (延喜式神名帳), a book from the mid-10th century; at that time, the shrine was [[Mino Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya (disambiguation)|''ichinomiya'']]. In [[1871]], it was called '''Nangū Shrine''' (南宮神社 ''Nangū Jinja''), but was promoted to a ''taisha'' (大社 ''grand shrine'') in [[1925]]. Its name was changed to Nangū Taisha after [[World War II]] to reflect its promotion. ==References== {{reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Visitor attractions in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[ja:南宮大社]]

revision.comment: expanded history
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = | width = 250 | caption = | type = | dedication = | founded = [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s reign | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1734-1 Miyashiromine
[[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine was first built during [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s reign, who lived from [[711 BCE]] to [[585 BCE]]. During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in [[1600]], the shrine was completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until [[1642]], when [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] led the construction efforts. In [[1867]], as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location. That temple is now named [[Shinzen-in]] (真禅院) and is still located in Tarui. This shrine was recorded in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'' (延喜式神名帳), a book from the mid-10th century; at that time, the shrine was [[Mino Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya (disambiguation)|''ichinomiya'']]. In [[1871]], it was called '''Nangū Shrine''' (南宮神社 ''Nangū Jinja''), but was promoted to a ''taisha'' (大社 ''grand shrine'') in [[1925]]. Its name was changed to Nangū Taisha after [[World War II]] to reflect its promotion. ==References== {{reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Visitor attractions in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[ja:南宮大社]]

This shrine was recorded in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'' (延喜式神名帳), a book from the mid-10th century; at that time, the shrine was [[Mino Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya (disambiguation)|''ichinomiya'']]. In [[1871]], it was called '''Nangū Shrine''' (南宮神社 ''Nangū Jinja''), but was promoted to a ''taisha'' (大社 ''grand shrine'') in [[1925]]. Its name was changed to Nangū Taisha after [[World War II]] to reflect its promotion. ==References== {{reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Visitor attractions in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[ja:南宮大社]]

南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | width = 250 | caption = Nangū Taisha | type = | dedication = | founded = [[Emperor Jimmu]]'s reign (ca. 711 to 585 BCE) | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1734-1 Miyashiromine

That temple is now named [[Shinzen-in]] (真禅院) and is still located in Tarui. This shrine was recorded in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'' (延喜式神名帳), a book from the mid-10th century; at that time, the shrine was [[Mino Province]]'s [[Ichinomiya (disambiguation)|''ichinomiya'']]. In 1871, it was called '''Nangū Shrine''' (南宮神社 ''Nangū Jinja''), but was promoted to a ''taisha'' (大社 ''grand shrine'') in 1925. Its name was changed to Nangū Taisha after [[World War II]] to reflect its promotion. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{commonscat|Nangu-taisha|Nangū Taisha}} {{coord missing|Gifu Prefecture}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Visitor attractions in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[ja:南宮大社]]

During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] led the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha]. revision.comment: expand
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kamiyama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. ==Background== The shrine is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] of Mino Province. The ''[[kami]]'' of the shrine is Kamiyama-hiko (金山彦命), the ''kami'' of mining and the metas industry. The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], and is collectively a national [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]]. The Hong of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==History== The shrine claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records, prior to its mention in the 836 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]].'' The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a major shrine. During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha]. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location. That temple is now named Shinzen-in (真禅院) and is still located in the town of Tarui. In 1871, the shrine was ranked as a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社|National shrine of the 2nd rank}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Taisha''|国幣大社|National shrine of the 1st rank}}in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisho" to reflect its more important status. ==Gallery== file:Nangu-taisha (Haiden).jpg|'''Haiden'''（ICP） file:Nangu-taisha (Romon).jpg|'''Romon'''（ICP） file:Nangu Taisha Kobuden.jpg|'''Kobuden'''（ICP) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nangu-taisha|Nangū Taisha}} *[http://www.nangu-san.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Tarui, Gifu]] [[Category:Mino Province]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. ==Background== The shrine is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] of Mino Province. The ''[[kami]]'' of the shrine is Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命), the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], and is collectively a national [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]]. The Hong of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==History== The shrine claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 AD ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]].'' The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a major shrine. During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha]. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location. That temple is now named Shinzen-in (真禅院) and is still located in the town of Tarui. In 1871, the shrine was ranked as a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社|National shrine of the 2nd rank}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Taisha''|国幣大社|National shrine of the 1st rank}}in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. ==Gallery== file:Nangu-taisha (Haiden).jpg|'''Haiden'''（ICP） file:Nangu-taisha (Romon).jpg|'''Romon'''（ICP） file:Nangu Taisha Kobuden.jpg|'''Kobuden'''（ICP) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nangu-taisha|Nangū Taisha}} *[http://www.nangu-san.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Tarui, Gifu]] [[Category:Mino Province]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the [[Kokufu|provincial capital]] of Mino Province. The ''[[kami]]'' of the shrine is Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命), the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], and is collectively a national [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]]. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location. That temple is now named Shinzen-in (真禅院) and is still located in the town of Tarui. In 1871, the shrine was ranked as a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Chūsha''|国幣中社|National shrine of the 2nd rank}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Taisha''|国幣大社|National shrine of the 1st rank}}in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. ==Gallery== file:Nangu-taisha (Haiden).jpg|'''Haiden'''（ICP） file:Nangu-taisha (Romon).jpg|'''Romon'''（ICP） file:Nangu Taisha Kobuden.jpg|'''Kobuden'''（ICP) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nangu-taisha|Nangū Taisha}} *[http://www.nangu-san.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Tarui, Gifu]] [[Category:Mino Province]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]]

The ''[[kami]]'' of the shrine is Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命), the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], and is collectively a national [[Important Cultural Property of Japan]]. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a major shrine. During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Nang%C5%AB_Taisha_shrine_,_%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE_-_panoramio_(27).jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den（ICP） File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon（ICP） File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii（ICP） File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge（ICP） 樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) 高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * Historical documents of Nangū Taisha (623 volumes) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Nangu-taisha|Nangū Taisha}} *[http://www.nangu-san.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nangu Taisha}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Tarui, Gifu]] [[Category:Mino Province]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Nang%C5%AB_Taisha_shrine_,_%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE_-_panoramio_(27).jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den（ICP） File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon（ICP） File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii（ICP） File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge（ICP） 樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Nang%C5%AB_Taisha_shrine_,_%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE_-_panoramio_(27).jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den（ICP） File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon（ICP） File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii（ICP） File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge（ICP） 樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) 高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * Historical documents of Nangū Taisha (623 volumes) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==External links== Prefecture]] [[Category:Tarui, Gifu]] [[Category:Mino Province]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

revision.comment: Moving [[:Category:Beppo Shrines]] to [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2022 June 2#Category:Beppo Shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Nang%C5%AB_Taisha_shrine_,_%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE_-_panoramio_(27).jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den（ICP） File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon（ICP） File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii（ICP） File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge（ICP） 樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) 高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * Historical documents of Nangū Taisha (623 volumes) ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|Kokuhei Chūsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Nang%C5%AB_Taisha_shrine_,_%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE_-_panoramio_(27).jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den（ICP） File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon（ICP） File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii（ICP） File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge（ICP） 樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) 高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese
