{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== * '''''[[Honden]]''''' (Main shrine), built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1069|title=南宮神社 本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Heiden (Shinto)|'''''Heiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1070|title=南宮神社 幣殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Haiden (Shinto)|'''''Haiden''''']], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1071|title=南宮神社 拝殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Kairō|'''Cloister''']], built in 1642. {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1077|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1078|title=南宮神社 回廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kairō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Chokushi-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1079|title=南宮神社 勅使殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Chokushi-den|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''''Kobu-den''''', built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1080|title=南宮神社 高舞殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Kobuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Rōmon]]'''''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1081|title=南宮神社 楼門|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''[[Mikoshi]]''' Garage''|楼門||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1082|title=南宮神社 神輿舎|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinyosha|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo| '''''Shinkanro'''''|神官廊||}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1083|title=南宮神社 神官廊|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Shinkanro|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Juge Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1072|title=南宮神社 摂社樹下神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Juge Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1073|title=南宮神社 摂社高山神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Takayama Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''Sub-shrine Hayato Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1074|title=南宮神社 摂社隼人神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Hayato Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Nandai Jinja Honden'''{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1075|title=南宮神社 摂社南大神神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Shichi-ōji Jinja Honden''' {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1076|title=南宮神社 摂社七王子神社本殿|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Sessha Sandai Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [[Torii|'''Stone Torii''']], built in 1642 and located in [[Tarui-juku]], built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1086|title=南宮神社 鳥居|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Torii|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Ring Bridge'''|輪橋|Rinkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1084|title=南宮神社 輪橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Rinkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''Gekō Bridge'''|下向橋|Gekōkyō}}, built in 1642.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1085|title=南宮神社 下向橋|trans-title=Nangū Taisha Gekōkyō |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''[[Tachi]]''', signed Yasumitsu, donated by Toki Yoriyoshi in 1398{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6180|title=太刀〈銘康光／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Tachi''', [[Heian period]], signed by Munechika Sanjo{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6181|title=太刀〈銘三条／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Halberd''' (2 pcs), [[Nara period]]{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/10378|title=鉾〈無銘／〉|trans-title=Tachi |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gifu Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nangū Taisha
南宮大社 | image = Nangu-taisha shrine.jpg | caption = Nangū Taisha | map_type = Japan Gifu Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|21|39.5|N|136|31|31.1|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:10000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Nangū Taisha | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Kanayama-hiko (金山彦命) | founded_by = | established = c. [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC) | date_destroyed = | location = 1734-1 Miyashiromine, Tarui-cho, Gifu-ken Japan | website = {{Official website|1=http://www.nangu-san.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 5 | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} [[File:Nanguh shrine(4).jpg|thumb|right|Nangū Taisha]] {{nihongo|'''Nangū Taisha'''|南宮大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the town of [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui]] in [[Fuwa District, Gifu|Fuwa District]], [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Mino Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 25.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine precincts contain 18 structures from the [[Edo period]], which are designated national [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The main building of the shrine is rebuilt every 51 years. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nangū Taisha is: * {{nihongo|'''Kanayama-hiko-no-mikoto'''|金山彦命|}}, the ''kami'' of mining and the metals industry. ==History== Nangū Taisha is located in the southwestern corner of Gifu Prefecture, at the foot of Mount Nangū. The name "Nangū" derives from its location to the south of the ancient [[Mino Provincial Capital]]. Nangū Taisha claims to have been first built during the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Sujin]] (97 BC – 30 BC), although there are no historical records prior to its mention in the 836 ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' and in the 859 ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]''. The mid-[[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' confirmed its status as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taisha''|名神大社}}. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1501, which destroyed all its records. It was rebuilt by [[Toki Masafusa]], the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province in 1511.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the nearby [[Battle of Sekigahara]] in 1600, the shrine was again completely burnt to the ground and was not rebuilt until 1642, when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] sponsored the construction efforts.[http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm Nangū Taisha] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090208211957/http://www.kaiun-goriyaku.com/21j-019.htm |date=2009-02-08 }}. Zenkoku Kaiun Jinja Bukkaku Guide. Accessed May 9, 2009. The shrine retains a document which details the cost of the reconstruction. In 1867, as a result of ''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'', the [[Japanese Buddhism|Buddhist temple]] located on the shrine grounds was moved to a different location and named Shinzen-in (真禅院). In 1871, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd rank|国幣中社|[[Kokuhei Chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] of [[State Shinto]]. It was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 1st rank|国幣大社|Kokuhei Taisha}} in 1925, and its name was changed from "Jinja" to "Taisha" to reflect its more important status. The shrine is located approximately one kilometer southwest of [[Tarui Station (Gifu)|Tarui Station]] on the [[JR Tokai]] [[Tōkaidō Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} == Nangū Otabi Shrine == {{Nihongo|Nangū Otabi Shrine|南宮御旅神社|Nangū Otabi Jinja}} is the [[Soja Shrine]] of [[Mino Province]]. It enshrines all fhe deities of the province Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} == Gallery == File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (27).jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:南宮大社 舞殿.JPG|Kobu-den (ICP) File:南宮大社 楼門.JPG|Rōmon (ICP) File:南宮大社 石造鳥居.JPG|Stone Torii (ICP) File:NanguuTaisya1.jpg|Entry File:南宮大社 大鳥居.JPG|Great Torii File:Nangū Taisha shrine , 南宮大社 - panoramio (12).jpg|Ring Bridge (ICP) File:樹下神社と隼人神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Juge Shrine & Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICPs) File:高山神社 (南宮大社境内社).JPG|Sub-shrine Takayama Shrine (ICP) File:Nanguu-otabi-jinja, haiden.jpg|link=|Soja shrine Nangu Otabi Shrine ==Cultural Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * '''Sub-shrine Takayama Jinja Honden'''{{cite web
